A [general election](/wiki/United_Kingdom_general_elections_overview "United Kingdom general elections overview") was held in the United Kingdom on Thursday 11 June 1987, to elect [650 members](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 1987 United Kingdom general election") to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). The election was the third consecutive general election victory for the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), who won a majority of 102 seats and second [landslide](/wiki/Landslide_victory "Landslide victory") under the leadership of [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher"), who became the first Prime Minister since the [Earl of Liverpool](/wiki/Robert_Jenkinson,_2nd_Earl_of_Liverpool "Robert Jenkinson, 2nd Earl of Liverpool") in [1820](/wiki/1820_United_Kingdom_general_election "1820 United Kingdom general election") to lead a party into three successive electoral victories.

The Conservatives ran a campaign focusing on lower taxes, a strong economy and strong defence. They also emphasised that unemployment had just fallen below the 3 million mark for the first time since 1981, and inflation was standing at 4%, its lowest level since the 1960s. National newspapers also continued to largely back the Conservative government, particularly *[The Sun](/wiki/The_Sun_(United_Kingdom) "The Sun (United Kingdom)")*, which ran anti–[Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") articles with headlines such as "Why I'm backing Kinnock, by [Stalin](/wiki/Stalin "Stalin")".[[1]](#cite_note-2)

Labour, led by [Neil Kinnock](/wiki/Neil_Kinnock "Neil Kinnock") following [Michael Foot](/wiki/Michael_Foot "Michael Foot")'s resignation in the aftermath of the party's landslide defeat at the [1983 general election](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election"), was slowly moving towards a more [centrist](/wiki/Centrist "Centrist") policy platform, following the promulgation of a [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing "Left-wing") one under Foot's leadership. The main aim of the Labour Party was to re-establish itself as the main progressive [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left "Centre-left") alternative to the Conservatives, after the rise of the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(UK) "Social Democratic Party (UK)") (SDP) forced Labour onto the defensive; and Labour succeeded in doing so at this general election. The [Alliance](/wiki/SDP%E2%80%93Liberal_Alliance "SDP–Liberal Alliance") between the SDP and the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") was renewed, but co-leaders [David Owen](/wiki/David_Owen "David Owen") and [David Steel](/wiki/David_Steel "David Steel") could not agree whether to support either major party in the event of a [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament").

The Conservatives were returned to government, having suffered a net loss of only 21 seats, which left them with 376 MPs and a reduced but still strong majority of 102 seats. Labour succeeded in resisting the challenge by the SDP–Liberal Alliance to maintain its position as HM Official Opposition. Moreover, Labour managed to increase its vote share in Scotland, Wales and the North of England. Yet Labour still returned only 229 MPs to Westminster; and in certain London constituencies which Labour had held before the election, the Conservatives actually made gains.

The election was a disappointment for the Alliance, which saw its vote share fall and suffered a net loss of one seat as well as former SDP leader [Roy Jenkins](/wiki/Roy_Jenkins "Roy Jenkins") losing his seat to Labour. This led to the two Alliance parties merging completely soon afterwards to become the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"). In Northern Ireland, the main unionist parties maintained their alliance in opposition to the [Anglo-Irish Agreement](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Agreement "Anglo-Irish Agreement"); however, the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") (UUP) lost two seats to the [Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party") (SDLP). One of the UUP losses was former Cabinet Minister [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell"), famous for his stance against immigration, and formerly a Conservative MP.

To date the Conservatives have not matched or surpassed their 1987 seat total in any general election held subsequently, although they recorded a greater share of the popular vote in the [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"). This Parliament, the 50th of its kind, would also be the last time that a Conservative government has lasted a full term with an overall majority of seats in Parliament, until the 2015-17 parliament.

The election night was covered live on the BBC, presented by [David Dimbleby](/wiki/David_Dimbleby "David Dimbleby"), [Peter Snow](/wiki/Peter_Snow "Peter Snow") and [Robin Day](/wiki/Robin_Day "Robin Day").[[2]](#cite_note-3) It was also broadcast on ITV, presented by Sir [Alastair Burnet](/wiki/Alastair_Burnet "Alastair Burnet"), [Peter Sissons](/wiki/Peter_Sissons "Peter Sissons") and [Alastair Stewart](/wiki/Alastair_Stewart "Alastair Stewart").

The 1987 general election saw the election of the first [Black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people") Members of Parliament: [Diane Abbott](/wiki/Diane_Abbott "Diane Abbott"), [Paul Boateng](/wiki/Paul_Boateng "Paul Boateng") and [Bernie Grant](/wiki/Bernie_Grant "Bernie Grant"), all as representatives for the Labour Party. Other newcomers included future Cabinet members [David Blunkett](/wiki/David_Blunkett "David Blunkett"), [John Redwood](/wiki/John_Redwood "John Redwood"), [John Reid](/wiki/John_Reid,_Baron_Reid_of_Cardowan "John Reid, Baron Reid of Cardowan"), [Andrew Smith](/wiki/Andrew_Smith_(British_politician) "Andrew Smith (British politician)") and [Alistair Darling](/wiki/Alistair_Darling "Alistair Darling"), future Shadow Cabinet minister [Ann Widdecombe](/wiki/Ann_Widdecombe "Ann Widdecombe"), and future [SNP Leader](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Scottish_National_Party "Leader of the Scottish National Party") [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond"). MPs who left the House of Commons as a result of this election include former Labour Prime Minister [James Callaghan](/wiki/James_Callaghan "James Callaghan"), [Keith Joseph](/wiki/Keith_Joseph "Keith Joseph"), [Jim Prior](/wiki/Jim_Prior "Jim Prior"), [Ian Mikardo](/wiki/Ian_Mikardo "Ian Mikardo"), former SDP leader and Labour Cabinet Minister [Roy Jenkins](/wiki/Roy_Jenkins "Roy Jenkins"), former Health Minister [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell") (who had defected to the UUP in Northern Ireland from the Conservatives in 1974) and [Clement Freud](/wiki/Clement_Freud "Clement Freud").

## Campaign and policies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1987_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Campaign and policies")]

|  |
| --- |
| * [1979 election](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election") * [MPs](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 1979 United Kingdom general election") |
| * [1983 election](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election") * [MPs](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 1983 United Kingdom general election") |
| * 1987 election * [MPs](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 1987 United Kingdom general election") |

The [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") campaign emphasised lower taxes, a strong economy and defence, and also employed rapid-response reactions to take advantage of [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") errors. [Norman Tebbit](/wiki/Norman_Tebbit "Norman Tebbit") and [Saatchi & Saatchi](/wiki/Saatchi_%26_Saatchi "Saatchi & Saatchi") spearheaded the Conservative campaign. However, when on "Wobbly Thursday" it was rumoured a Marplan opinion poll showed a narrow 2% Conservative lead, the "exiles" camp of [David Young](/wiki/David_Young,_Baron_Young_of_Graffham "David Young, Baron Young of Graffham"), [Tim Bell](/wiki/Tim_Bell "Tim Bell") and the advertising firm [Young & Rubicam](/wiki/Young_%26_Rubicam "Young & Rubicam") advocated a more aggressively anti-Labour message. This was when, according to Young's memoirs, Young grabbed Tebbit by the lapels and shook him, shouting: "Norman, listen to me, we're about to lose this fucking election."[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampbell2003522-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) In his memoirs, Tebbit defends the Conservative campaign: "We finished exactly as planned on the ground where Labour was weak and we were strong—defence, taxation, and the economy."[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETebbit1988336-6) During the election campaign, however, Tebbit and party leader [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") argued.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThatcher1993584-7)

Bell and Saatchi & Saatchi produced memorable posters for the Conservatives, such as a picture of a British soldier's arms raised in surrender with the caption "Labour's Policy On Arms"—a reference to Labour's policy of [unilateral nuclear disarmament](/wiki/Unilateral_nuclear_disarmament "Unilateral nuclear disarmament"). The first Conservative [party political broadcast](/wiki/Party_political_broadcast "Party political broadcast") played on the theme of "Freedom" and ended with a fluttering Union Jack, the hymn *[I Vow to Thee, My Country](/wiki/I_Vow_to_Thee,_My_Country "I Vow to Thee, My Country")* (which Thatcher would later quote in her "[Sermon on the Mound](/wiki/Sermon_on_the_Mound "Sermon on the Mound")") and the slogan "It's Great To Be Great Again".

The Labour campaign was a marked change from previous efforts; professionally directed by [Peter Mandelson](/wiki/Peter_Mandelson "Peter Mandelson") and [Bryan Gould](/wiki/Bryan_Gould "Bryan Gould"), it concentrated on presenting and improving [Neil Kinnock](/wiki/Neil_Kinnock "Neil Kinnock")'s image to the electorate. Labour's first party political broadcast, dubbed *Kinnock: The Movie*, was directed by [Hugh Hudson](/wiki/Hugh_Hudson "Hugh Hudson") of *[Chariots of Fire](/wiki/Chariots_of_Fire "Chariots of Fire")* fame, and concentrated on portraying Kinnock as a caring, compassionate family man. It was filmed at the Great Orme in Wales and had "[Ode to Joy](/wiki/Ode_to_Joy "Ode to Joy")" and Brahms' [1st Symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._1_(Brahms) "Symphony No. 1 (Brahms)") as its music.[[7]](#cite_note-8) He was particularly critical of the high unemployment that the government's economic policies had resulted in, as well as condemning the wait for treatment that many patients had endured on the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service"). Kinnock's personal popularity jumped 16 points overnight following the initial broadcast.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButlerKavanagh1988154-9)

On 24 May, Kinnock was interviewed by [David Frost](/wiki/David_Frost "David Frost") and claimed that Labour's alternative defence strategy in the event of a Soviet attack would be "using the resources you've got to make any occupation totally untenable".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In a speech two days later Thatcher attacked Labour's defence policy as a programme for "defeat, surrender, occupation, and finally, prolonged guerrilla fighting ... I do not understand how anyone who aspires to Government can treat the defence of our country so lightly".[[9]](#cite_note-10)

During the 1987 election campaign the Conservative Party issued attack posters which claimed that the Labour Party wanted the book *[Young, Gay and Proud](/wiki/Young,_Gay_and_Proud "Young, Gay and Proud")* to be read in schools, as well as *Police: Out of School*, *The Playbook for Kids about Sex*,[[b]](#cite_note-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12)[[11]](#cite_note-13) and *The Milkman's on his Way*,[[c]](#cite_note-14) which, according to the [Monday Club](/wiki/Monday_Club "Monday Club")'s [Jill Knight](/wiki/Jill_Knight "Jill Knight") [MP](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament") – who introduced [Section 28](/wiki/Section_28 "Section 28") and later campaigned against [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage")[[12]](#cite_note-15) – were being taught to "little children as young as five and six", which contained "brightly coloured pictures of little stick men showed all about homosexuality and how it was done", and "explicitly described homosexual intercourse and, indeed, glorified it, encouraging youngsters to believe that it was better than any other sexual way of life".[[13]](#cite_note-Hansard1-16)

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1987_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Timeline")]

The Prime Minister [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") visited [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") on 11 May and asked the Queen to [dissolve Parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Dissolution of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") on 18 May, announcing that the election would be held on 11 June. The key dates were as follows:[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-19)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Monday 18 May | Dissolution of the [49th Parliament](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 1983 United Kingdom general election") and campaigning officially begins |
| Wednesday 10 June | Campaigning officially ends |
| Thursday 11 June | Polling day |
| Friday 12 June | The Conservative Party wins with a majority of 102 to retain power |
| Wednesday 17 June | [50th Parliament](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 1987 United Kingdom general election") assembles |
| Thursday 25 June | [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") |

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1987_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Results")]

Further information: [Results of the 1987 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/Results_of_the_1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "Results of the 1987 United Kingdom general election")

The Conservatives were returned by a second [landslide victory](/wiki/Landslide_victory "Landslide victory") after their first in 1983,[[17]](#cite_note-20) with a comfortable majority, down slightly on 1983 with a swing of 1.5% towards Labour. This marked the first time since the [Great Reform Act of 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") that a party leader had won three consecutive elections, although the Conservatives had won consecutively in the 1950s under three different leaders ([Churchill](/wiki/Churchill "Churchill") in [1951](/wiki/1951_United_Kingdom_general_election "1951 United Kingdom general election"), [Eden](/wiki/Sir_Anthony_Eden "Sir Anthony Eden") in [1955](/wiki/1955_United_Kingdom_general_election "1955 United Kingdom general election") and [Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan") in 1959) and early in the century, the Liberals also had three successive wins under two leaders ([Henry Campbell-Bannerman](/wiki/Henry_Campbell-Bannerman "Henry Campbell-Bannerman") in 1906 and [H. H. Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith") twice in 1910). The Conservative lead over Labour of 11.4% was the second-greatest for any party since the Second World War; surpassed only by the previous 1983 result.[[18]](#cite_note-TimesGuide1987p253-21)

The BBC announced the result at 02:35. Increasing [polarisation](/wiki/Political_polarization "Political polarization") marked divisions across the country; the Conservatives dominated [Southern England](/wiki/Southern_England "Southern England") and took additional seats from Labour in London and the rest of the South, but performed less well in [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), Scotland and Wales, losing many of the seats they had won there at previous elections. Yet the overall result of this election proved that [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher")'s policies retained significant support.

Despite initial optimism and the professional campaign by [Neil Kinnock](/wiki/Neil_Kinnock "Neil Kinnock"), the election brought only twenty additional seats for Labour from the 1983 Conservative landslide. In many southern areas, the Labour vote actually fell, with the party losing seats in London. However, it represented a decisive victory against the SDP–Liberal Alliance and marked out the Labour Party as the main contender to the Conservative Party. This was in stark contrast to 1983, when the Alliance votes almost matched Labour; although Labour had almost 10 times as many seats as the Alliance due to the structure of the First-Past-The-Post voting system.

The result for the Alliance was a disappointment, in that they had hoped to overtake Labour as the Official Opposition in the UK in terms of vote share. Instead, they lost [Roy Jenkins](/wiki/Roy_Jenkins "Roy Jenkins")' seat and saw their vote share drop by almost 3%, with a widening gap of 8% between them and the Labour Party (compared to a 2% gap four years before). These results would eventually lead to the end of the Alliance and the birth of the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)").

Most of the prominent MPs retained their seats. Notable losses included: [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell") (the controversial former Conservative Cabinet Minister who had defected to the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party")), [Gordon Wilson](/wiki/Gordon_Wilson_(Scottish_politician) "Gordon Wilson (Scottish politician)") (leader of the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party")) and two Alliance members: Liberal [Clement Freud](/wiki/Clement_Freud "Clement Freud") and former SDP leader [Roy Jenkins](/wiki/Roy_Jenkins "Roy Jenkins") (a former Labour Home Secretary and Chancellor of the Exchequer). [Neil Kinnock](/wiki/Neil_Kinnock "Neil Kinnock") increased his share of the vote in [Islwyn](/wiki/Islwyn_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Islwyn (UK Parliament constituency)") by almost 12%. [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") increased her share of the vote in her [Finchley](/wiki/Finchley_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Finchley (UK Parliament constituency)") seat, but the Labour vote increased more, slightly reducing her majority.

In Northern Ireland, the various unionist parties maintained an [electoral pact](/wiki/Electoral_pact "Electoral pact") (with few dissenters) in opposition to the [Anglo-Irish Agreement](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Agreement "Anglo-Irish Agreement"). However, the Ulster Unionists lost two seats to the [Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party").

The Conservatives' victory could also arguably be attributed to the rise in [living standards](/wiki/Living_standards "Living standards") during their time in office. As noted by [Dennis Kavanagh](/wiki/Dennis_Kavanagh "Dennis Kavanagh") and [David Butler](/wiki/David_Butler_(psephologist) "David Butler (psephologist)"):

> Since 1987 the Conservatives had located a large constituency of "winners", people who have an interest in the return of a Conservative government. It includes much of the affluent South, home-owners, share-owners, and most of those in work, whose standard of living, measured in post-tax incomes, has risen appreciably since 1979.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButlerKavanagh1988277-22)
